demo-videos.title: Product Demo Videos
demo-videos.description: How to script, narrate, caption, and pace a product demo video that people actually watch — with the numbers (length, word rates, retention) instead of vibes.

demo-automation.title: Demo Automation
demo-automation.description: Demos and screenshots that keep themselves up to date — why product media rots, and how CI can re-render it from a spec when the product changes.

ai-demo-tools.title: Demo Tools & Alternatives
ai-demo-tools.description: The demo-tooling landscape mapped honestly: AI demo generators, screen recorders, interactive demo platforms — what each mechanism can and cannot do.

demos-as-code.title: Demos as Code
demos-as-code.description: Treating the demo as a committed artifact: agent-authored storyboards, deterministic browser replay, multi-language and per-prospect renders from one recording.

screen-recording.title: Screen Recording
screen-recording.description: The practical mechanics of a clean screen capture — resolution, framerate, format, cursor, notifications, audio, and doing it on Linux or with no display at all — answered with settings tables instead of vibes.

video-marketing.title: Video Marketing for Software
video-marketing.description: Where a demo video meets distribution: landing pages, email, YouTube, Product Hunt, social, and search — one recording turned into every placement, with the format, length, and metric each channel actually wants.

video-docs.title: Video Documentation & Tutorials
video-docs.description: Video as a documentation medium: when a clip beats a paragraph, how microvideos and GIFs live in docs sites, and how to keep a tutorial library from lying about the product as the product ships.

topics.title: How to make a product demo video (that people finish)
keywords: how to make a product demo video
topics.angle: The end-to-end playbook — script first, record second — with concrete budgets (words per second of narration, seconds per scene, total length by channel) and where each step can be automated instead of performed.
evidenceHooks: Wistia video-length engagement data (wistia.com/learn)
HubSpot video marketing statistics report
W3C WCAG 2.2 captions success criteria
vendor docs for at least two recording tools, dated

topics.title: Demo video script template: problem, walkthrough, proof
topics.angle: A copy-pasteable script skeleton with per-section word budgets derived from speech rate (~2.5 words/sec English TTS and human narration), plus two worked examples — one SaaS dashboard, one dev tool.
evidenceHooks: published speech-rate research or broadcasting style guides (words per minute)
Wistia/Vidyard guidance on demo structure, dated
a real conference-talk or launch-video transcript to dissect

topics.title: How long should a demo video be? The retention math
keywords: how long should a demo video be
topics.angle: Length by placement (landing hero, README, sales follow-up, docs) anchored in published engagement curves — and why the answer is usually 'shorter than you recorded', which is an editing problem tools can solve.
evidenceHooks: Wistia engagement-by-length data
Vidyard video benchmarks report
YouTube/LinkedIn native-video guidance, dated

topics.title: AI voiceover for demo videos: quality, cost, and workflow
topics.angle: What current TTS actually sounds like for product narration, what it costs per finished minute (with arithmetic), where it beats recording yourself (iteration, localization, consistency) and where it still loses (emotion, brand voice).
evidenceHooks: OpenAI TTS pricing/docs, dated
an open-weights TTS project (e.g. Kokoro) README
ElevenLabs pricing page, dated

topics.title: Captions on demo videos: the accessibility case and the numbers
topics.angle: Most demo views start muted — captions are the difference between a view and a bounce. WCAG requirements, word-level vs block timing, open vs closed captions for embeds, and how caption generation fits an automated pipeline.
evidenceHooks: W3C WCAG captions success criteria (1.2.2)
a published muted-autoplay / captions-usage statistic with a traceable source
Whisper (openai/whisper) paper or repo for word-level timing

topics.title: What makes a screen recording look professional
topics.angle: Deconstruct the polish bar set by tools like Screen Studio into its mechanics — zoom-on-interaction, cursor smoothing, dead-time trimming, sizing/letterboxing — and show each one is a deterministic transform on footage, not taste.
evidenceHooks: Screen Studio feature docs, dated
ffmpeg zoompan/crop filter docs
a published UI-motion guideline (e.g. Material motion durations)

topics.title: SaaS demo video examples: what the best ones do differently
topics.angle: Close-read 6-8 publicly linkable SaaS demo videos (timestamps, structure, narration density) and extract the repeatable patterns — an analysis piece, not a mood board.
evidenceHooks: publicly hosted demo videos that can be linked and timestamped
Wistia/Vidyard benchmark data for comparison

topics.title: Automated product demo videos: regenerate, don't re-record
topics.angle: The taxonomy of what 'automated' actually means across the market — AI-polished human takes, clip re-capture with propagation, synthetic compositions, and spec-driven replay of a real UI — and which failure (staleness) each one does or does not solve.
evidenceHooks: Trainn's own 'no re-recording needed' marketing page, dated
charmbracelet/vhs README (terminal demos as code, the proven pattern)
GitHub Actions scheduled-workflow docs

topics.title: Why every product demo is out of date (and what rot costs)
topics.angle: The pain piece: ship velocity vs media half-life. Estimate rot rates from public changelogs (UI-visible changes per month for a few well-known SaaS), tally the re-record cost, and frame regeneration-from-spec as the only mechanism that scales.
evidenceHooks: public changelogs of 2-3 fast-shipping SaaS products (count UI-visible changes)
simonw's shot-scraper writeup on automating screenshots
Trainn marketing page naming the same pain, dated

topics.title: Rendering demo videos in CI: a GitHub Actions walkthrough
topics.angle: The engineering how-to: what a video-rendering CI job needs (browser, ffmpeg, fonts, headless quirks), how to trigger on product change vs cron, and how to publish the artifact (commit, release asset, stable embed URL) — tool-agnostic, with one concrete worked pipeline.
evidenceHooks: GitHub Actions runner-image software list (what ubuntu-latest actually ships)
charmbracelet/vhs GitHub Action docs
GitHub docs on raw file URLs / release assets

topics.title: README GIFs that update themselves
topics.angle: The README demo GIF as a maintained artifact: size/framerate budgets GitHub actually renders well, GIF vs MP4 vs animated WebP tradeoffs, stable raw-URL embedding, and a cron-recapture loop so the GIF never lies about the product.
evidenceHooks: GitHub docs on images in READMEs and camo caching
charmbracelet/vhs (tape files for terminal GIFs)
ffmpeg palettegen/paletteuse docs for quality GIF encoding

topics.title: Automating product screenshots for docs and marketing
topics.angle: Stills are the gateway drug to demo automation: shot-scraper, Heroshot-style cron→re-capture→PR loops, screenshot golden-diffing, and when a set of named stills beats a video entirely.
evidenceHooks: shot-scraper docs / simonwillison.net writeup
Heroshot README (cron re-capture + auto-PR workflow)
Playwright screenshot API docs

topics.title: AI demo video generators: what the label actually covers
keywords: ai video generator for software demos
topics.angle: Buyers searching this phrase get four unrelated mechanisms sold under one label: AI-polished human recordings, interactive tour builders with AI copy, synthetic text-to-video, and agent-authored replay of a real UI. Map the mechanisms to use cases so the reader can stop comparing apples to screenshots.
evidenceHooks: vendor docs/pricing for one tool per mechanism, dated
G2 Demo Automation category page (breadth of the label)
Remotion 'agent era' positioning page

topics.title: Screen Studio alternatives for Windows, Linux, and CI
topics.angle: Screen Studio set the polish bar but is a macOS-only GUI (Windows 'in review' for years, subscription-only since late 2025). Honest per-platform alternatives table — Cap, OBS+post, Tella, aidemo (disclosed as ours), and others verified live — scored on the polish features people actually cite.
evidenceHooks: Screen Studio pricing page and platform status, dated
Cap (cap.so) repo/docs
each listed alternative's own docs, verified live

topics.title: Open-source demo video tools, compared by mechanism
topics.angle: The genuinely-open landscape: Cap (recorder), vhs (terminal tapes), Remotion's license fine print (source-available ≠ open source), Revideo, editly, aidemo — with install friction and license terms checked against the actual repos.
evidenceHooks: Remotion license page (company license terms), dated
OSI open-source definition
each tool's repo license file, verified live

topics.title: Interactive product demos vs video demos: when each wins
topics.angle: Tour platforms (Storylane, Navattic, Arcade) capture HTML and replay clicks; videos carry narration and ship anywhere an or
evidenceHooks: Storylane/Navattic/Arcade docs on what their captures are, dated
G2 category review-volume data
a published buyer-enablement or PLG report with traceable sourcing

topics.title: Demos as code: commit the storyboard, render the video
topics.angle: The category-defining piece: what changes when the demo is a committed spec instead of a captured performance — diffable, reviewable, regenerable, agent-authorable. The terminal world proved it with vhs; the browser is the harder, more valuable version.
evidenceHooks: charmbracelet/vhs README and its golden-file testing story
infrastructure-as-code literature for the analogy (e.g. HashiCorp docs)
Remotion/Revideo positioning pages (the synthetic-composition contrast)

topics.title: Letting a coding agent make your demo video
topics.angle: The agent workflow in practice: the agent explores the app, writes the storyboard (script + selectors), a deterministic engine records — and why keeping the LLM out of the capture loop is the design decision that makes the output shippable.
evidenceHooks: MCP specification docs (modelcontextprotocol.io)
Remotion Agent Skills / Revideo agent positioning, dated
Claude Code skills/plugins docs

topics.title: Deterministic browser automation for video capture
topics.angle: What it takes to replay the same browser flow into the same footage twice: wait strategies vs sleeps, animation and clock control, viewport/DPR pinning, and why flakiness that merely annoys a test suite ruins a video.
evidenceHooks: Playwright docs on auto-waiting and video recording
Playwright/Chromium docs on reduced motion or animation control
a published flaky-test study or engineering writeup with traceable sourcing

topics.title: Video-as-code tools: Remotion, Revideo, editly, vhs — and real-UI capture
topics.angle: The programmatic-video landscape split by what the pixels are: synthetic compositions (Remotion, Revideo), composed pre-existing media (editly), terminal replay (vhs), and real-product capture — with licenses and install friction checked against the repos.
evidenceHooks: Remotion license terms, dated
editly repo (native-deps build status)
vhs repo
Revideo repo

topics.title: Multi-language product demo videos from one recording
topics.angle: Localization economics: re-recording per locale vs re-voicing one take. TTS + caption realignment makes N locales a rendering matrix, with the caveats (UI language vs narration language, word-rate differences by language) treated honestly.
evidenceHooks: published speech-rate-by-language data with traceable sourcing
OpenAI TTS or another multi-locale TTS vendor's language docs, dated
W3C i18n or a localization industry source on video localization cost

topics.title: Personalized demo videos at scale: the template-render pattern
topics.angle: Per-prospect demo videos without per-prospect recording: parameterize the storyboard (name, data, flow), render the matrix, and the honest limits — personalization that changes the UI state vs a name overlay.

published data on personalization response rates with traceable sourcing

topics.title: Product onboarding videos that shorten time-to-value
topics.angle: A taxonomy of onboarding-video jobs (welcome, first-run walkthrough, feature-unlock nudge, empty-state prompt) mapped to the activation metric each one moves, with a length budget per type and the time-to-value arithmetic behind why a focused 2-minute walkthrough beats a 20-minute tour.

topics.title: The async sales demo video: a repeatable asset, not a live call

topics.title: Product launch video: one recording, every launch-day cut

topics.angle: A licensing table (royalty-free libraries, YouTube Audio Library, Creative Commons) with the actual terms, plus the one number that separates amateur from pro: ducking music to roughly -18 to -20 LUFS under narration so the voice stays intelligible, with the loudness arithmetic.

topics.title: The demo video thumbnail: poster frame vs marketing cover

topics.title: Product demo storytelling: three frameworks on one timeline

topics.title: B2B vs PLG demo videos: seven axes that change the cut

topics.title: Screen recording without the mouse cursor (or with a better one)

topics.title: Video SEO for product pages: VideoObject schema, done right
topics.angle: A copy-pasteable VideoObject JSON-LD template with the properties Google actually requires (name, description, thumbnailUrl, uploadDate, contentUrl), how it nests inside Product schema, and the transcript/caption practices that make video content indexable, with the rich-result CTR data.

topics.title: Social cuts for a launch: one demo into a week of clips
topics.angle: Turn one demo into a launch's worth of social clips: a per-platform cut table (aspect, length, caption-burn, hook) for LinkedIn, X, and TikTok/Reels/Shorts, plus the editing rule set (cold open, burned captions, 9:16 reframe) so you're not re-recording per network.

topics.angle: LinkedIn-specific mechanics for B2B software: native upload beats a YouTube link for reach, square/vertical beats 16:9 in-feed, muted autoplay demands burned captions and a text hook, with a length/spec table and the document-carousel alternative for non-video weeks.

topics.title: Does video actually increase conversion? Tracing the 80% claim
keywords: does video on landing page increase conversion
topics.angle: An honest read of the 'video lifts conversion 80%' claims: trace the widely-cited stats to their source, separate correlation from causation, and give the conditions under which video actually helps (complex product, fast load, relevant content) vs when it backfires (heavy hero, slow LCP).

topics.title: How to embed video on a website: self-host vs YouTube vs streaming host
keywords: how to add video to website

topics.title: Where to use your demo video: one recording, ten placements
keywords: how to use a product demo video
topics.angle: A distribution checklist that turns one recording into ten placements (landing hero, README, docs, onboarding email, sales follow-up, Product Hunt, LinkedIn, app store, changelog, YouTube), each with the format, length, and aspect it needs — the reuse map that makes a demo video pay for itself.

topics.title: Visual regression testing for marketing assets, not just components
topics.angle: Engineering teams pixel-diff their component library but let the homepage, pricing page, and demo stills drift unchecked. A taxonomy of marketing assets worth baselining (full-page screenshots, hero stills, embedded demo frames) mapped to the diff tool that fits each, plus the threshold/anti-aliasing settings that stop a font-render change from failing every build.
evidenceHooks: Percy docs on baselines and per-snapshot diff review (percy.io)
BackstopJS README (full-page/section diffing for CMS pages)
Chromatic docs on visual review and diff thresholds

topics.title: Keeping App Store screenshots current with automation
topics.angle: The arithmetic App Store submission forces on you: locales times device sizes times shots is hundreds of PNGs that go stale every redesign. Walk the fastlane snapshot + frameit pipeline (UI-test-driven capture, device frames, localized captions from JSON), do the combinatorics on a real matrix, and show when a browser-capture path beats the simulator path.
evidenceHooks: fastlane snapshot action docs (docs.fastlane.tools/actions/snapshot)
fastlane frameit docs (device frames + caption JSON)
Apple App Store Connect screenshot specifications / requirements

topics.title: Release-notes videos generated per release
topics.angle: A monthly recap video that a human edits is a chore that gets skipped; a per-tag video rendered from a spec is a CI artifact. Decision table for which releases earn a video (visual/material vs API/bugfix), a sustainable cadence backed by published practice, and how to wire generation to the same git tag the written changelog already uses.
evidenceHooks: Keep a Changelog specification (keepachangelog.com)
Appcues release notes examples and cadence guidance
Clueso 'release-note videos vs changelogs' head-to-head, dated

topics.title: Docs that test themselves: executable documentation
topics.angle: The doctest idea (examples in the docs run as tests, so prose can't drift from behavior) generalized from code snippets to product media: a screenshot or demo that re-captures the live UI is a doctest for the interface. Survey the executable-docs patterns that already ship (Python doctest, Sphinx, Rust/Elixir doctests) and map each to its media equivalent.
evidenceHooks: Python doctest module docs (docs.python.org/3/library/doctest)
Sphinx doctest extension docs (sphinx-doc.org)
ExUnit.DocTest docs (Elixir doctests from @doc)

topics.title: Demo environments and seed data that make a demo reproducible
topics.angle: A demo is only regenerable if the state behind it is. The mechanics of a deterministic demo backend: a fixed RNG seed so the same rows appear every run, believable-but-PII-free personas, and reset-to-clean scripting, with a worked seed script and the failure modes (timestamps that age, external API calls that vary) that silently break replay.
evidenceHooks: Prisma seeding docs (deterministic seed scripts)
Faker.js README (seeded, reproducible fake data)
Bunnyshell demo-environments page on pre-loaded data, dated

topics.title: Versioned demos: one demo per product release
topics.angle: Treat the demo like a release artifact pinned to a version tag: v2.3's demo shows v2.3, and old versions keep their old demo instead of silently being overwritten. How to key demos to SemVer, publish them as release assets, and give docs a stable per-version URL, with the storage math for keeping N versions around.
evidenceHooks: Semantic Versioning specification (semver.org)
GitHub docs on release assets and downloads
charmbracelet/vhs (tape files versioned alongside code)

topics.title: Nightly demo builds: re-render the demo while you sleep
topics.angle: Borrow the nightly-build habit from compilers: a scheduled job re-records the demo against the current app so drift surfaces the next morning, not in a customer call. The GitHub Actions cron wiring, how to fail the job on a visual diff instead of just publishing, and cost math for running it nightly vs on-merge.
evidenceHooks: GitHub Actions scheduled workflows / cron trigger docs
charmbracelet/vhs GitHub Action docs
GitHub Actions runner billing / minutes documentation

topics.title: Detecting UI drift before your demo lies about the product
topics.angle: Drift detection sits between never noticing and re-recording everything: what signal to watch (DOM/selector presence, full-page pixel diff, layout-shift metrics) and how to tune it so a copy tweak doesn't page you but a moved primary button does. A ladder from cheap DOM assertions to perceptual diffing, with the false-positive tradeoff at each rung.
evidenceHooks: Visualping guide to visual regression monitoring / detecting UI changes
Percy visual diff docs (intelligent diffing vs pixel-matching)
arXiv paper on AI for context-aware visual change detection in test automation (2405.00874)

topics.title: Generating demo data that looks real (and stays safe)
topics.angle: Lorem-ipsum data makes a demo look fake; production data makes it a compliance incident. The middle path: seeded synthetic data with coherent relationships (names that match avatars, dates that make a timeline, foreign keys that resolve) using Faker-class generators, plus a checklist for scrubbing real data down to demo-safe.
evidenceHooks: Faker.js README (locales, seeding, deterministic output)
Python Faker documentation (providers, reproducible seeds)
SeedBase / FK-consistent synthetic data generator docs

topics.title: Visual regression testing tools compared by diff engine
topics.angle: Cut the crowded category by the thing that actually differs: the diff engine and where baselines live. Table of pixel diffs (pixelmatch, odiff), DOM/perceptual hybrids (Percy, Chromatic, Applitools), and framework-native runners (Playwright toHaveScreenshot, Vitest) scored on flakiness handling, review UX, and self-hosted vs SaaS baseline storage.
evidenceHooks: Playwright toHaveScreenshot / visual comparison docs
BackstopJS and pixelmatch repos (open-source pixel diffing)

topics.title: Loom alternatives for product demos after the Atlassian era
topics.angle: Loom is a talking-head async recorder that Atlassian bought and re-priced; using it as a polished product-demo tool means fighting its grain. Sort the alternatives by what they actually replace (async messaging vs polished demo vs interactive tour vs code-native render), with Loom's real free-plan caps and each alternative's verified live, aidemo disclosed as ours.

topics.title: Arcade vs Navattic vs Storylane: picking an interactive demo platform
topics.angle: The three market leaders sell the same category with different centers of gravity: Navattic optimizes HTML-clone fidelity and analytics, Storylane optimizes speed and demo hubs, Arcade optimizes design polish and now video export. A decision matrix by capture type, pricing floor (Navattic's enterprise minimum vs Storylane's transparent tiers), and maintenance cost, with when a rendered video beats all three.

topics.title: Camtasia alternatives for software demos and tutorials
topics.angle: Camtasia is a heavyweight timeline editor priced as a perpetual/subscription desktop app; most people searching this want either a lighter recorder or a way to stop hand-editing every tutorial. Split alternatives into record-and-edit (Cap, ScreenPal), AI-assisted docs (Guidde, Clueso), and code-native render, scored on license cost and whether editing is manual or regenerable. aidemo disclosed as ours.

A matrix of output type, edit-after-record friction, and how each handles the doc going stale, with where an agent-authored render fits. aidemo disclosed as ours.

topics.title: Free demo video software: what the free tier actually gives you
topics.angle: 'Free' hides three different deals: truly free/open (OBS, Cap), free-tier-with-caps (Loom's video limit, ScreenPal's 15-minute cap, watermarks), and free-trial. topics.title: Synthesia alternatives when the job is a software demo, not an avatar
topics.angle: Avatar video (Synthesia, HeyGen, Colossyan) is built for a talking head reading a script, which is the wrong tool when the point is showing the product. Where avatars genuinely fit software (training, compliance, multi-language talking-head) vs where product-native capture wins, with a decision line drawn on output type rather than avatar quality. aidemo disclosed as ours.

topics.angle: The most-searched Screen Studio head-to-head: Cap (open source, cross-platform, cheaper) vs Screen Studio (macOS-only, the polish benchmark). topics.title: OBS Studio for product demos: what it does and doesn't do
topics.angle: OBS is free, open-source, and captures anything, but it is a live-broadcast tool: no auto-zoom, no cursor smoothing, no dead-time trimming, and every take is a manual performance. A build-vs-buy accounting of what OBS gives free, what you rebuild by hand in post, and when the manual-take cost exceeds a polished or code-native tool. aidemo disclosed as ours.

topics.title: Descript alternatives for editing demo videos
topics.angle: Descript's hook is transcript-based editing (delete a word, delete the footage); alternatives split into other text-based editors, timeline editors, and the option to not edit at all because the demo re-renders from a spec. A table by editing model, filler-word/overdub features, and export limits, with the honest case for and against editing-by-transcript. aidemo disclosed as ours.

topics.title: Storyboard design patterns for demos as code
topics.angle: When the demo is a JSON/spec file, storyboard authoring becomes a design-patterns problem: scene granularity, one-assertion-per-beat, separating narration from action-spec, and keeping selectors out of the prose. A catalog of reusable patterns and anti-patterns (the mega-scene, the sleep-driven wait, the hardcoded index) drawn from real declarative-demo formats. aidemo storyboard used as a worked example, disclosed as ours.
evidenceHooks: charmbracelet/vhs tape syntax docs (the proven declarative format)

topics.title: Reviewing video and media diffs in pull requests
topics.angle: A binary MP4 shows as 'Binary file changed' in a diff, which is useless for review. The techniques that make a media change reviewable: diff the spec not the pixels, attach a rendered preview to the PR, use GitHub's image diff modes (2-up, swipe, onion-skin) for stills, and gate merge on a visual-diff check. A checklist of what a reviewer actually approves.
evidenceHooks: GitHub docs on viewing image diffs in pull requests (2-up/swipe/onion-skin)
charmbracelet/vhs docs on tape review and golden output
Chromatic docs on UI review and PR checks

topics.title: Testing demos like code: golden files and drift gates
topics.angle: If a demo is code, it gets a test suite: a golden capture committed as the baseline, a CI job that re-renders and diffs, and a review gate to bless intended changes. Map the golden-file / snapshot-testing pattern (vhs, Jest snapshots, Playwright toHaveScreenshot) onto product media, including how to make renders deterministic enough that the golden actually holds.
evidenceHooks: charmbracelet/vhs golden-file / output testing docs
Jest snapshot testing documentation (jestjs.io)
Playwright toHaveScreenshot / visual comparison docs

topics.title: MCP servers for media generation: the agent-to-render interface
topics.angle: How a coding agent should talk to a render pipeline: the Model Context Protocol job model (submit spec, poll status, fetch artifact) versus a synchronous tool call, why long media renders demand async jobs and progress, and what a good media MCP surface exposes (validate, probe, render, artifacts). aidemo's MCP used as a worked example, disclosed as ours.
evidenceHooks: Model Context Protocol specification (modelcontextprotocol.io)
MCP docs on tools/resources and long-running operations
Remotion agent skills / an existing media MCP server, verified live

topics.title: Selectors that survive a redesign
topics.angle: A demo script pinned to nth-child and CSS class hashes breaks the day a designer touches the layout. The selector-resilience ladder (role/accessible-name, text, test-id, structural CSS) ranked by how well each survives a visual redesign, with concrete before/after examples of a redesign that breaks fragile selectors and leaves resilient ones untouched.
evidenceHooks: Testing Library guiding principles / 'which query' priority (getByRole first)
Playwright locators best-practices docs (user-facing/role/test-id)
Cypress best-practices docs on selecting elements

topics.title: Versioning media assets in git: LFS, committed, or external
topics.angle: If demos are code, where do the rendered MP4s live? A three-way decision (commit the binary, Git LFS, or store externally and commit only the spec) with the real numbers: LFS storage/bandwidth quotas and overage pricing, repo-bloat math from committing binaries, and why committing the spec while treating the render as a rebuildable artifact often wins.
evidenceHooks: Git LFS project site (git-lfs.com) on pointer files
GitHub docs on Git LFS storage and bandwidth quotas / billing
GitLab LFS docs (.gitattributes tracking of binary assets)

topics.title: Agent-friendly app instrumentation: data-testid and beyond
topics.angle: Small instrumentation choices decide whether an agent can script your UI reliably: prefer accessible roles/names, add data-testid only where semantics run out, keep values stable and lint for orphans. A short instrumentation playbook that pays off for tests, agents, and demo capture at once, with the anti-patterns (testid tied to styling, duplicated ids) called out.
evidenceHooks: Testing Library docs on ByRole vs ByTestId priority and rationale
Playwright test-id docs (configurable data-testid attribute)
WAI-ARIA roles documentation (accessible names as durable hooks)

topics.title: Reproducible demo renders: byte-for-byte, on purpose
topics.angle: Golden-file testing only works if the same spec renders the same bytes twice, which media pipelines fight by default (embedded timestamps, font-render variance, non-pinned encoders). Apply reproducible-builds discipline to a render: SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH, stripped metadata, pinned ffmpeg/fonts, and output hashing, with the specific non-determinism sources ranked by how often they bite. aidemo's golden/probe flow as a worked example, disclosed as ours.
evidenceHooks: Reproducible Builds project docs (reproducible-builds.org, SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH)
ffmpeg docs on metadata / -map_metadata and encoder determinism
charmbracelet/vhs golden-output approach

topics.title: Video documentation for software: when the medium earns its keep
topics.angle: The end-to-end case for video as a documentation medium, decided by evidence not enthusiasm: where video measurably outperforms text (procedural, first-run, spatial UI) and where it loses (lookup, search, maintenance), a format decision table across the doc surface (onboarding, KB, API, changelog), and the maintenance tax that sinks most tutorial libraries unless the video regenerates.
evidenceHooks: arXiv 'Software Professionals' Attitudes towards Video as a Medium in Requirements Engineering' (1809.00804)
Nielsen Norman Group research on video content and usability
Help Scout guide to building a video knowledge base

topics.title: Video vs written documentation: the decision, with evidence
topics.angle: Not a versus, a routing decision. A rubric that maps each doc job to a format on four axes (task type, lookup vs learn, maintenance cost, searchability), grounded in the software-engineering research on video as a documentation medium rather than the usual 'people love video' stat, and honest that text still wins on SEO and quick reference.
evidenceHooks: arXiv 'Software Professionals' Attitudes towards Video as a Medium in Requirements Engineering' (1809.00804)
arXiv 'Software Professionals are Not Directors: What Constitutes a Good Video?' (1808.04986)
Nielsen Norman Group articles on video usability and scanning behavior

topics.title: Microvideos in documentation: the 10-second clip that replaces a paragraph
topics.angle: The unit that actually works in docs is not the tutorial, it is the 5-15 second silent clip showing one action (the HelpVid pattern). Where microvideos slot into the Diataxis quadrants (how-to and reference, not explanation), a length/format budget, and why a library of tiny clips is more maintainable than a few long walkthroughs.
evidenceHooks: HelpDocs blog on 'HelpVids' (short, silent, single-action clips)
Diataxis documentation framework (diataxis.fr) on doc types
ClickHelp guidance on video in technical documentation

topics.title: GIFs in documentation: when a loop beats a screenshot
topics.angle: The GIF is the most abused format in docs: autoplaying, unstoppable, huge, and inaccessible. When a GIF genuinely beats a still or a video, the file-size math (why an MP4 is often 10x smaller than the same GIF), the accessibility rules an auto-looping GIF violates (WCAG pause/stop, prefers-reduced-motion), and the encode settings that keep it small.
evidenceHooks: WCAG 2.2 Success Criterion 2.2.2 Pause, Stop, Hide (moving content)
MDN prefers-reduced-motion documentation
ffmpeg palettegen/paletteuse docs (quality vs size) and GitHub image-in-docs docs

topics.title: In-app onboarding walkthrough videos that survive redesigns
topics.angle: Onboarding walkthroughs are the highest-value and fastest-rotting doc video: they show the exact first-run UI, which is exactly what changes most. Where a video beats an interactive product tour for first-run, the structure that survives small UI changes (narrate outcomes, not pixel positions), and the regeneration loop that keeps the walkthrough matching today's signup flow.
evidenceHooks: Nielsen Norman Group research on onboarding and first-time user experience
Appcues onboarding documentation/examples, dated
arXiv research on what makes a good software how-to video (1808.04986)

topics.title: Support and knowledge-base videos that actually deflect tickets
topics.angle: A KB video only pays off if it deflects a ticket, which most don't because they are too long, unsearchable, or stale. What earns a video in a help center (repeated, procedural, high-volume tickets), the pair-with-text rule for search and muted viewing, and how to instrument deflection so you can tell which videos work, backed by the preference stats with their real source.
evidenceHooks: Help Scout guide to a video knowledge base (tips, tools, examples)
Wyzowl State of Video Marketing report (video-preference statistics), dated
a help-desk vendor's docs on video in KB articles (Zendesk/Intercom/HappyFox), verified live

topics.title: Keeping a tutorial video library current at scale
topics.angle: The reason teams stop making doc videos: 200 tutorials times a UI redesign equals an un-fundable re-record backlog. Estimate the maintenance debt from real ship velocity, contrast the strategies (accept rot, prune aggressively, or regenerate from spec), and show why a library only stays honest if its videos rebuild from source. aidemo used as a worked example, disclosed as ours.
evidenceHooks: public changelogs of 2-3 fast-shipping SaaS products (UI-visible change rate)
simonwillison.net writeup on automating screenshots with shot-scraper
GitHub Actions scheduled-workflow docs (cron re-render)

topics.title: Developer-education screencasts: terminal, code, and browser together
topics.angle: Teaching developers has its own capture problem: readable code, real terminal output, and a browser, none of which a generic recorder handles well. A format guide for dev screencasts (font sizes that survive compression, terminal replay with asciinema/vhs vs raw capture, when to show code vs narrate it) and how to keep a code-heavy screencast from going stale the next release.
evidenceHooks: asciinema documentation (terminal session recording as text)
charmbracelet/vhs README (scripted terminal screencasts)
arXiv 'Software Professionals are Not Directors' on what makes a good dev video (1808.04986)

topics.title: Embedding video in docs sites without wrecking load time
topics.angle: The docs-framework how-to nobody writes: self-host vs YouTube-embed vs GIF, lazy-loading so a video-heavy page still passes Core Web Vitals, MP4 vs WebM, and the exact embed syntax for Docusaurus (MDX), MkDocs Material, and plain HTML. A performance-and-portability table, with poster frames and preload settings that matter.
evidenceHooks: Docusaurus docs on MDX and embedding media
MkDocs Material documentation on embedding video
MDN <video> element docs and web.dev guidance on lazy-loading video

topics.title: Accessible tutorial videos: captions, transcripts, audio description
topics.angle: Doc video accessibility is more than captions: transcripts (which also give you the SEO text a video lacks), audio description for UI-only moments, and keyboard-operable players. Map each WCAG media criterion to a concrete tutorial requirement and show the transcript doubling as searchable documentation, so accessibility and findability are the same work.

W3C WAI 'Making Audio and Video Media Accessible' guide

topics.title: Measuring whether documentation videos actually work
topics.angle: Views are a vanity metric for a doc video; the questions are 'did it deflect a ticket' and 'did people finish the part that matters'. A metric stack for doc video (completion and drop-off curves, ticket-deflection rate, search-to-video ratio) with definitions and how to instrument each, so you can retire the videos nobody watches instead of maintaining them forever.
evidenceHooks: Wistia engagement / play-rate and video-analytics documentation
a help-desk vendor's definition of self-service / ticket deflection rate (Zendesk/Intercom), verified live
Nielsen Norman Group research on video engagement and drop-off

topics.title: API and SDK documentation videos that stay in sync with the API
topics.angle: Quickstart videos for APIs rot faster than any other doc video because a renamed field or changed response breaks them silently. What belongs in an API-docs video (the shape of a call and response, not a 20-minute tour), how to capture terminal + code + JSON legibly, and why an API video should regenerate from the same fixtures the docs are tested against.
evidenceHooks: asciinema docs (recording terminal API calls as replayable text)
a first-party API-docs example that uses video quickstarts (Stripe or Twilio docs), verified live
OpenAPI Specification (the source of truth an API video should track)
